coredata.dc:title: DFT study of 2D graphitic carbon nitride based preferential targeted delivery of levosimendan, a cardiovascular drug
coredata.dc:creator: Qusain Afzal, Qaba
Rafique, Javeria
Jaffar, Kinza
Perveen, Mehvish
Iqbal, Javed
Al-Buriahi, M.S.
Alomairy, Sultan
Alrowaili, Z.A.
Somaily, H.H.
coredata.dc:description: 2D nanocarriers particularly graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) have shown its momentum in nanomedicine to augment the efficacy and safety of pharmaceutical drugs owing to its excellent compatibility and nontoxic nature. In current study, DFT/TD-DFT evaluation is performed for a cardiovascular drug levosimendan, g-C3N4 carrier and their complex g-C3N4-levosimendan at CAMB3LYP/6-31G (++) ** with a high precision of 0.1 eV in charge transfer. To study the nature of the complex different parameters such as non-covalent interaction analysis, density of states, charge decomposition analysis, natural bonding orbital analysis, electrostatic potential and UV–Visible spectra are investigated. The BSSE corrected adsorption energy of complex has confirmed an exothermic reaction indicating successful adsorption of levosimendan on g-C3N4 while solvation energy has described a good solubility index and stability for the complex. All the chemical reactivity parameters indicate a favorable complex having a potential of preferential targeting to the heart tissue. Dipole moment shows a significant increase for complex indicating its high solubility in water while NBO studies has signified the more pronounced interactions where the E value is larger for levosimendan and g-C3N4 which is further supported by ELF and ESP plots. Moreover, less distortion of bonds has been seen with +1 and −1 charges on the surface of g-C3N4-levosimendan-complex. PET based on electron-hole theory specifies g-C3N4 as a chelator while levosimendan as a fluorophore in the g-C3N4-levosimendan complex (also confirmed by UV–Visible results). All the results are in a good support of the complex which signify that g-C3N4 offers an excellent route of administration for levosimendan and has a potential to spark active research in nanomedicine particularly in targeted drug delivery systems.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: 2D nanocarriers particularly graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) have shown its momentum in nanomedicine to augment the efficacy and safety of pharmaceutical drugs owing to its excellent compatibility and nontoxic nature. In current study, DFT/TD-DFT evaluation is performed for a cardiovascular drug levosimendan, g-C3N4 carrier and their complex g-C3N4-levosimendan at CAMB3LYP/6-31G (++) ** with a high precision of 0.1 eV in charge transfer. To study the nature of the complex different parameters such as non-covalent interaction analysis, density of states, charge decomposition analysis, natural bonding orbital analysis, electrostatic potential and UV–Visible spectra are investigated. The BSSE corrected adsorption energy of complex has confirmed an exothermic reaction indicating successful adsorption of levosimendan on g-C3N4 while solvation energy has described a good solubility index and stability for the complex. All the chemical reactivity parameters indicate a favorable complex having a potential of preferential targeting to the heart tissue. Dipole moment shows a significant increase for complex indicating its high solubility in water while NBO studies has signified the more pronounced interactions where the E value is larger for levosimendan and g-C3N4 which is further supported by ELF and ESP plots. Moreover, less distortion of bonds has been seen with +1 and −1 charges on the surface of g-C3N4-levosimendan-complex. PET based on electron-hole theory specifies g-C3N4 as a chelator while levosimendan as a fluorophore in the g-C3N4-levosimendan complex (also confirmed by UV–Visible results). All the results are in a good support of the complex which signify that g-C3N4 offers an excellent route of administration for levosimendan and has a potential to spark active research in nanomedicine particularly in targeted drug delivery systems.

ce:section.ce:para: When a novel therapeutic drug is discovered, there arises a need for enhanced routes of administration as well as a better scientific perception about how drug delivery affects safety profile [2]. Basically it is not a drug itself but the way of administration [3] which decides the therapeutic efficacy of a drug. Drug pharmacokinetics (PK), accumulation, delivery, metabolism, period of medicinal effect, absorption, and health risks are all affected by the method of administration used [4]. Drug administration is not an easy task. Nano-delivery being an effective emerging technique is used for targeted drug delivery to improve biomedical functions of many potential drugs [5]. Introduction of nano systems for targeted drug delivery has a reign to change the landscape of a drug to overwhelm the lack of specificity at the target site [6], high cytotoxicity [7], solubility issue [8], and degradation of a therapeutic agent. Last thirty years of advancements in nanotechnology [9] and drug delivery [10] has shown major developments in medicine, resulting in the rise of nanomedicine as a new field [11]. This versatility and immensity of nanotechnology has directed many researchers to develop nanoparticle-based drug delivery system [12,13]. Different mechanisms of drug delivery have been evolved to enhance drug choice and avoid adverse effects [14]. Numerous properties of drug molecules, such as preferential targeting [15,16], cellular absorption [17], biodistribution, solubility, circulation time [18], and pharmacokinetics is a major concern in nanomedicine.

It is hard to estimate the rates of drug release, cell and tissue-controlled targeting and drug stabilization. Drug delivery intended to boost therapeutic efficacy by stabilizing it in vivo, monitoring its release as well as localizing its effects [19]. Effectiveness of many drugs is hampered by their off-target outcomes and safety, as well as poor solubility after their systemic administration. For example, most cardiovascular drugs are untargeted and may have noxious compounds which can carry out unwanted functions and often incapacitating side effects [20]. For such drugs, nanomaterials are engineered which have the power to enhance pharmacokinetic properties of the drugs. Drug delivery system helps to minimize the harmful effects of various drugs including cardiovascular drugs [21]. It helps by distributing drugs to the desired location or infected location in a predictable rate. A vector is required for a successful targeted drug delivery which must not be toxic and have a proper binding ability. This problem can be solved by using nanocarriers which can transport active ingredients with improved permeability, on-target delivery [22], and increased solubility [23].

Nanocarriers [24] are a paradigm of nanotechnology and got tremendous interest in the field of Nanomedicine [25]. The pattern of nanocarriers is considerably critical in nanomedicine. Investigators mainly concentrated on the creation of innovative and effective nano-carriers to minimize side effects and increase drug therapeutic scale [26]. It is their submicron size which make their off-loading easier at the targeted site and make them long-time circulating. 2D nanocarriers [27] has proven their efficacy in preferential drug targeting. These include fullerenes, phosphorene, graphene, and carbon nano tubes [28]. In these years, graphene holds a strong spot with respect to all carbon-based materials because of its exceptional optical and electric properties [29]. In a 2D (2-dimenssional) world another competitor, graphitic carbon nitride [30] has come out as an important derivative of C3N4 (carbon nitride) [31] having unique properties and good biocompatibility with several applications in nanomedicine [32]. The g-C3N4 exhibits high steadiness, non-toxicity and is taken into consideration as a promising catalyst for sustainable development because of high catalytic activity under the electromagnetic radiation [33]. The nanosheets of graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) have been recorded to be effective photosensitizers [34] and drug nanocarriers for drug delivery [35]. Additionally, g-C3N4 nanosheets have an extremely high load carrying capacity due to their high surface to volume ratio and a bad gap of 2.7 eV [36]. Moreover, toxicity of g-C3N4 towards biological system is very low as it consist of s constituents e.g., carbon and nitrogen which ensures its metal free nature (as metals can cause the damage to body-cells) [37], low toxicity, and increased bioavailability [38]. It has good bi-compatibility for human body and can be metabolized easily inside the biological system. These intrinsic features of g-C3N4 have attracted the great attention of biological researchers.

The weak intermolecular interactions between the individual components of complex ensure successful drug release from the carrier at the target site. Mostly, London dispersion forces are effective for a good drug release profile [39]. The drug release depends upon the adsorption energy value. The adsorption energies of about −1.3 eV (−30 kcalmol−1) [40] are suitable for drug-loading at the carrier surface or slow release of drug at the targeted area. Environmental factors such as the ionic interaction between the drug-molecule and the secondary substances can also alter the rate of drug-release. Furthermore, the degradation of nanocarrier, the solubility of drug in fats and water, the ability to bind with protein, pH and photothermal effect can be key factors for controlling the drug release profile [41].

The drug-carrier complex is driven in human body by the assistance of some important parameters including size, shape, temperature, pH and the location of a molecule. So, PCT and PET [42] process are considered beneficial for drug-carrier complex to check the UV–Visible spectra, fluorescence quenching/enhancement effect and related dynamics on the overall system.

In the queue of various cardiovascular drugs, calcium sensitizers [43] are proven to be more effective than calcium mobilizers. Among the three known calcium sensitizers levosimendan [44] is recognized by its pharmacodynamics and pharmacokinetic properties along with its therapeutic upshots which improve the cardiac efficiency and heart rate [45]. It is used to treat decompensated congestive heart failure. Levosimendan improves cardiac ischemia without increasing oxygen requirements and induces cardiac and systemic vasodilation at recommended levels [46]. Other findings have revealed that levosimendan increases respiratory muscle strength, as well as it gives multi-organ functions [47] including the protection of liver and kidney [48]. However, its efficacy is restricted because of its toxicity and reduced favorable targeting to the specific heart tissue [49]. In this research, computational work for the cardiovascular therapeutic drug (levosimendan) is performed for the first time by the most reliable method of DFT and TD-DFT and the loading capacity of graphitic carbon nitride as a potential carrier in drug delivery is evaluated.

All the computational results are accomplished by the Gaussian 09 software program [50]. The CAMB3LYP [51] function hybrid with the 6–31 G** (++) level (valence doubleζ polarized) basis set which is more useful and reliable for DFT calculations [52,53] is applied to optimize the structures. Although B3LYP is one of the well-known hybrid or exchange–correlation functional (with 20% HF exchange) which is good at explaining electronic-properties but it underestimate the prediction of dispersion and long-range interactions [54]. The dispersion-forces and long-range-correction play key role in non-covalent systems. Another hybrid-functional CAM-B3LYP with hybrid-qualities of long-range corrections and B3LYP is used. This new exchange–correlation functional, CAM-B3LYP (with 65% HF exchange at long-range and 35% Becke (1998) exchange can estimate long range corrections [55]. Furthermore, dispersion corrections can be measured by using Gremmi’s method [56].

Moreover, CAM-B3LYP which is a modified form of B3LYP is preferred over B3LYP as it shows a higher degree of precision of about 0.1 eV in charge transfer [57] and thus gives best results in levosimendan targeted drug delivery. TD-DFT is currently the key interest of many researchers [58] specifically for those whose major concern is in effective nano-mediated drug delivery. Basically TD-DFT helps in the prediction of effective parameters more precisely and explains the molecular behavior in biochemical systems more reliably [59]. So, in this computational study of a cardiovascular drug delivery, ground state geometric optimization and excited state properties of levosimendan, g-C3N4 and their complex g-C3N4-levosimendan are measured using density functional theory (DFT) and time dependent-density functional theory (TD-DFT) [60]. Frequency calculations were performed which confirm that the levosimendan-graphitic carbon nitride complex is at its local minima as no imaginary-frequencies were observed. Adsorption and solvation energies are also computed to check the reaction kinetics or feasibility of g-C3N4-levosimendan complex formation process. Adsorption energy is the root of complex formation as it explains the interaction between drug-carrier complex [61]. It is the difference between the complex energy and the sum of individual energies of graphitic carbon nitride and levosimendan. Hence, it is the free energy which describes the adsorption energies [62 62] of the individual or complex system. Adsorption energy for complex is obtained using the following equation [63]:

To attain a good estimation of adsorption energy of complex, basis set superposition error (BSSE) [64] correction is employed with counterpoise [65] method [66,67] (BSSE occurs because of the incomplete description of the basis set). BSSE corrected Ead [68] is calculated by using equation:

The CAM-B3LYP functional can estimate long-range corrections but fails to predict dispersion correction [69] which are the key factor in determining weak forces (Van der Waal forces). Thus, to study the long-range dispersion interactions energy correction is needed with DFT. So, to estimate dispersion corrections, Grimme’s method [56] is employed with CAM-B3LYP-D3 model. Energy calculation with dispersion correction in the studied system can be done on the basis of given relation [31].

is a dispersion correction in accordance to Grimme’s method.

Solvation energies are calculated to check the effect of solvent on complex stability [70]. The use of water in all the cells, tissues, and organs of a human body is substantial. Therefore, to examine the effect of water as a solvent on levosimendan, g-C3N4 and g-C3N4-levosimendan (complex), data obtained from integral equation formalism with polarizable-continuum model (IEFPCM) [71] are studied. Mathematically the ΔE solvation is explained as follows:
(4)
Δ E s o l v a t i o n = E s o l - E g a s
where ΔE solvation defines the solvation energy of the complex, Esol depicts the total energy values obtained from solvent and Egas explains the overall energy of the complex in gas phase. To explain the interaction of levosimendan and g-C3N4, we investigated important parameters like energies of HOMO-LUMO, bandgap, dipole moment, and λmax (maximum absorption wavelength). UV–Visible spectra and quantum stability parameters [72–74] for example chemical potential μ , softness S
[75], electrophilicity ω
[76] and chemical hardness η are also computed for solvent phase and gas phase separately using following equations.
(5)
E g
=
E
LUMO
-
E
HOMO
(6)
μ = - ( I + A ) / 2
(7)
S = 1 / η
(8)

2
/ 2 η
(9)
η = ( I - A ) / 2
where η is chemical hardness, S denotes chemical softness, μ is a chemical potential while ω represents electrophilicity index. In above equations, I denote “- ELUMO” and A represent “-EHOMO”. Other parameters of investigation include non-covalent interaction analysis (NCI) [77], electron localization function (ELF) [78,79], density of states (DOS) [80,81], photo-induced electron transfer (PET) [82,83], charge decomposition analysis (CDA) [84], and natural bonding orbital analysis [83 85]. VMD [86] and Multiwfn 3.7 (a multi-function wave-function analyzer) [87] are used to compute results. For UV–Visible plots Origin software is used [88,89]. Moreover, electrostatic potential [90,91] colored image has also been obtained to observe charge density at particular area. Effect of positive and negative charges on the surface of g-C3N4 and levosimendan-g-C3N4 complex is also explained in this study.

To compute adsorption energies, the carrier, drug and their complex are first optimized. Their optimized structures are demonstrated in Fig. 1 . The g-C3N4-levosimendan complex shows the non-bonding interaction of nitrogen and hydrogen where levosimendan drug serves as a contributor of N for NH bonding. During adsorption of levosimendan on g-C3N4, two nonbonding NH interactions with distances 4.97 Ao and 5.29 Ao; and one CH interaction with distance 4.60 has been developed. To check the feasibility of the complex formation, the BSSE corrected adsorption energy is calculated for the complex system which is −0.715 eV. The negative value of Ead confirms that no additional energy is required for the complex formation as the process is exothermic. It also indicates that the process of adsorption is energetically feasible as it is confined within the principles of thermodynamics. Moreover, Ead signifies the successful adsorption of levosimendan on graphitic carbon nitride, but this interaction is non-covalent which makes the offloading of levosimendan easier at the target site. As the adsorption energy value is found to be less than 1 eV, which suggests that the release of drug is easy at the target site. When the dispersion contribution (DFT-D) is calculated for complex-system, it comes out to be −3.12 eV. This clearly reflect that the dispersion-contribution is significant in the non-covalent complex system. These weak interactions also aid in successful offloading of levosimendan.

To study the humidity effect on adsorption, the Ead in the presence of water molecules is calculated. It has been seen that the BSSE corrected Ead (−0.326 eV) in the presence of water molecules which is less as compared to Ead (=0.715 eV) in gas, suggesting a decreased stability of complex in humid condition. It is also interesting to note that both the values are close to each other which confirms the complex-formation in both the phases, aqueous as well as gas phase.

Solvation energy is an important parameter which is computed to check the solubility of the complex. To check the solvent effect on g-C3N4-levosimendan complex, water is chosen as a solvent due to its importance in pharmaceutical formulations and in achieving desired pharmacological response in human body. Lesser the solvation energy greater will be the solubility of the complex. Solvation energies are calculated for g-C3N4, levosimendan and their complex. The solvation energy for the complex g-C3N4-levosimendan came out to be −1.843 eV while for levosimendan and g-C3N4 the value of solvation energy is equal to −0.597 eV and −1.586 eV, respectively. The negative values signify that this reaction is spontaneous. It is observed from the above results that our complex is stable and have a good solubility index.

HOMO-LUMO studies help us to comprehend the stability and the reactivity of the system. HOMO-LOMO gap indicates the transition energy required for successful interaction between drug and carrier. So, to evaluate the drug carrier interaction, the key factor is the energies associated with highest occupied molecular orbitals and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals of carrier, drug and their complex. Fig. 2 illustrates the visual representation of HOMO-LUMO orbitals of g-C3N4, levosimendan and g-C3N4-levosimendan complex. It is seen that for g-C3N4, the HOMO-LOMOs are uniformly dispersed on their surface. Same is the case with the HOMO of the drug while for drug, LUMO is pointed at one end. For complex molecule it can be seen from Fig. 2 that the LUMO is located on the drug while HOMO is at the surface of g-C3N4.

This clearly indicates the transfer of electrons from g-C3N4 to levosimendan as the energy of HOMO of complex is comparable to that of carrier as depicted from Table 1 . The g-C3N4 molecule shows energy value of −7.86 eV for HOMO and a value of −1.44 eV for LUMO with a band gap of 6.42 eV. In gas phase, levosimendan has shown a band gap of 6.26 eV with energy of −8.1 eV for HOMO and −1.86 eV for LUMO. In complex the band gap is of 6.15 eV with the HOMO-LUMO energy-values of −7.93 eV and −1.78 eV, respectively. This band gap variation demonstrates the red shift of 49 nm.

In solvent phase the same evaluation is done, and the results are illustrated in Table 2 and Fig. 3 . It is clearly seen that in solvent phase, the band gap of complex and drug is almost the same i.e., 6.17 so they show maximum absorption values at nearly the same wavelengths. While the band gap of g-C3N4 is 6.53 and g-C3N4-levosimedan complex showed a red shift of 74 nm.

Band gap energies obtained from HOMO-LUMO analysis not only defines the electron transition but also explains the softness/hardness and the quantum factors associated with the value of electrophilicity, chemical potential and dipole moments. For the preferential targeted delivery of a cardiovascular drug levosimendan with the help of g-C3N4, we also checked these parameters in both gas phase as well as solvent phase and their results are depicted in Table 3 and Table 4 . These chemical descriptors are commonly known as chemical reactivity parameters. It must be kept in mind that more the negative value of µ more will be the stability.

To evaluate the molecular orbital level contribution of levosimendan and g-C3N4 in their complex, charge decomposition analysis has been performed. The donor–acceptor interaction can be estimated from the visual expression of intermixing of molecular orbitals and the development of new MOs. It has also been seen that after the addition of new molecular orbitals in g-C3N4-levosimendan complex, the band gap energies reduce. This reduction is helpful for the successful charge transfer and consequently successful adsorption of the drug levosimendan on the carrier g-C3N4. Results of CDA analysis for g-C3N4-levosimendan complex are shown in Fig. 7 (a). To check the outcome of adsorption of levosimendan on g-C3N4 electronic properties, DOS spectrum has been plotted [92,93] as illustrated in Fig. 7(b) which also support CDA intermixing of molecular orbitals resulting in a band gap of 6.15 eV after the formation of more MOs during g-C3N4-levosimendan complex formation.

To investigate the interaction type between g-C3N4 and levosimendan in g-C3N4-levosimendan complex, non-covalent interaction analysis has been performed [94]. This is an important step in analyzing successful drug delivery at the target tissue as if the interaction of the levosimendan and g-C3N4 in a complex is stronger than the offloading of the drug at the carrier site is much difficult and vice versa. In this analysis, a plot is obtained between reduced density gradient and sinλ2 p which graphically describes the bonding type between drug and the carrier. This graph is shown in Fig. 8 . The isosurface image for RDG taken from VMD is also represented in Fig. 9 . This evaluation procedure is basically based upon electron densities and on the value of sinλ2 p. For instance, hydrogen-bonding occurs between the drug and the carrier if sinλ2 p is less than unity but if it is greater than unity, it highlights the steric hindrance while sinλ2 p = 0 indicates Van-der-Waal forces of attraction. The graph plotted for g-C3N4-levosimendan complex signposts all the three values which signify the non-covalent interaction between graphitic carbon nitride and levosimendan. Moreover, 3D (3-dimensional) visual representation of NCIs between g-C3N4 and levosimendan is obtained as shown in Fig. 9. In this image the blue color indicates hydrogen bonding while the green color highlights the Van-der-Waal forces between levosimendan and g-C3N4. The NCI plot clearly defines the weak interaction between Nitrogen and hydrogen which has also been seen in geometrical investigation of complex.

The bond as well as lone pair positioning are better understood with ELF. The two-dimensional image of colored locations based on the ELF results and density equal to the real electron density, generates a descriptive image which is shown in Fig. 10 . The key concern in ELF is where the electrons are localized. It is not where the electrons are; or in quantum words, where the electrons are projected to be, that are traced from electron density. Electron localization function is maximum where electrons show pairing. Clear differences can be seen in ELF diagrams of g-C3N4 before and after complex (g-C3N4-levosimendan) formation.

NBO analysis is conducted for the carrier g-C3N4 along with the complex g-C3N4-levosimendan at the CAM-B3LYP/6–31 G** level to elaborate intra and inter-molecular charge transfer dynamics as well as electron density delocalization inside the molecules is also analyzed. Second Order Perturbation Theory Analysis of Fock Matrix in NBO Basis provides us information about donor–acceptor molecules and charge transfer phenomena. Table 5 and Table 6 represent the highest perturbation energies for g-C3N4 and g-C3N4-levosimendan complex, respectively.

Table 7 gives the information about drug to carrier and carrier to drug charge transfers for levosimendan and graphitic carbon nitride. The NBO analysis for g-C3N4 carrier is also studied before and after complex formation and the results are displayed in Table 7. The intra- and intermolecular charge transfer interactions caused by electron density delocalization between occupied and unoccupied NBO orbitals lead to a stabilization of such associations where the E value is higher. Thus, the higher the E value, the more pronounced the interaction between levosimendan and g-C3N4. It means that there is a higher contributing tendency by electron donors to electron acceptors representing the more degree of conjugation of the entire structure as in π * C2-N 10 →
π
* C14 - N16, π * C 61 - C 62→ π * C 59 - C 60, π * C 33 - N 35→
π
* C34 - N36 and LP (2) N15→
π
*N9–C13, charge transfer demonstrate maximum energies indicating their stabilization as demonstrated in Table 6. This study of the natural bonding orbital [83] for levosimendan and g-C3N4 indicates a very precise natural molecular configuration of Lewis since all orbitals are used numerically to achieve their maximum electron density percent. Charge transfer from donor to acceptor levels: LP (1) N15 → σ* C83-H84. LP (1) N16 → σ* C83-H84, LP (1) N23 → σ* H57-N69, LP (2) N23 → σ* H57-N69 show energy at 0.53, 0.05, 0.17 and 1.13 kJ/mol which depicts the energies for NH interaction between g-C3N4 and the drug levosimendan. The above results are in a great agreement with ELF plots.

For photophysical properties of levosimendan, g-C3N4 and their complex (g-C3N4-Levosimendan), absorption spectra in the UV–Visible region are obtained. Fig. 11 represents the spectra taken in gas phase as well as in aqueous phase. In gas phase, λmax for levosimendan is 313 nm while for g-C3N4 it is about 264 nm. Maximum absorption for g-C3N4-levosimendan complex is seen at the wavelength of 313 nm parallel to 3.96 eV while its band ranges from 252 nm to 368 nm. This value of λmax corresponds to the HOMO-LUMO gap difference indicating a correlation between electronic transitions and maximum wavelength of absorption. This correlation confirms that the charge transfer phenomena has taken place. As the maximum absorption for g-C3N4 falls at 264 nm in gas phase while in aqueous phase it is 253 nm. Therefore, blue shift of 11 nm has been observed in the absorption spectra of g-C3N4 when solvent phase is compared with gaseous phase. In water solution, the maximum absorption values obtained for drug levosimendan and g-C3N4-levosimendan complex are 326 nm and 327 nm, respectively. These values for levosimendan are close to the previous studies with a range of 330–450 nm [45] while 200–450 nm range of absorption has been reported for g-C3N4 [95] which is also parallel to our results.

The interactions between a variety of chemical structures and various biological elements are mostly based upon the surface charges i.e., positive, neutral, or negative [96]. To investigate the upshots of + 1 and −1 charges and their impact on bond-lengths, the g-C3N4 and g-C3N4-levosimendan complex are optimized in their cationic and anionic forms. The bond lengths are evaluated for the optimized structures and a graph is developed between bond index (number of bonds) and bond-length. For a molecule of graphitic carbon nitride, the analysis with a positive charge has resulted in the bond lengths within a range of 1.38–1.40 A°, 1.30–1.37 A° and 1.0–1.01 A° associated with CN, CN, NH, respectively while CN, CN, NH bond lengths in the carrier with −1 charge has been noticed in a range of 1.35–1.42 A°, 1.29–1.40 A° and 1.00–1.01 A°, respectively.

It has been noticed that the adsorption of levosimendan on g-C3N4 with +1/−1 charge states gives a complex (g-C3N4 +1/-1-Levosimendan) having lower dissemination of bond lengths than that of g-C3N4 with +1/−1 charges. Fig. 12 shows bond lengths associated with g-C3N4 +1/-1 and its complex (g-C3N4 +1/-1-Levosimendan). In optimized structures of complex with +1/−1 charge states, three bonds are formed between hydrogen form the drug and nitrogen from the carrier. The complex (g-C3N4 +1-Levosimendan) shows three NH bonds having lengths of 2.65 A°, 2.79 A°, 3.34 Ao while graph of complex with a negative charge (g-C3N4 -1-Levosimendan) confirms three NH bonds formation having lengths of 2.61, 2.65, 2.75 Ao. From the graph it has been seen that for complex g-C3N4 -1-Levosimendan the bond lengths of NC, NN, NN and CO occur at 1.16, 1.36, 1.38 and 1.23 A°, respectively. While CC, CC, CN, CN, and CH show their bond lengths within a range of 1.38–1.41 A°, 1.49–1.53 A°, 1.30–1.39 A°, 1.37–1.40 A° and 1.08–1.09 A°, respectively. For complex g-C3N4 +1-Levosimendan the bond lengths of NC, NN, NN and CO occur at 1.15, 1.30, 1.38 and 1.23 A°, respectively. Moreover, the bond lengths of CC, CC, CN, CN, and CH have been noticed within a range of 1.38–1.42 A°, 1.48–1.53 A°, 1.28–1.39 A°, 1.36–1.41 A° and 1.08–1.09 A°, respectively.

For the computation analysis of Atomic dipole moment corrected Hirshfeld (ADCH) [97,98] graphs are plotted with the atomic index on y-axis and ADCH charges on x-axis for g-C3N4 +1-levosimendan complex as well as for g-C3N4 -1-levosimendan complex. These graphs demonstrate that the nitrogen charges fall in negative region while the ADCH values for carbon and hydrogen fall in the positive quadrant as exhibited in Fig. 13 . Moreover, for complex g-C3N4 -1-levosimendan, it has been noticed that carbon atoms present in levosimendan also fall in negative quadrant. In g-C3N4 -1-levosimendan, oxygen atom maintains a negative charge of −0.0051 a.u. while in g-C3N4 +1-levosimendan the ADCH charge value is −0.40 a.u. Thus, it is disclosed that the surface charge placement facilitates to find the active spots of a molecule. It also explains the nature of bonds and their specific sites.

One of the important analyses performed by using TD-DFT method is photo-induced electron and charge transfer. It is important in explaining fluorescence spectra including fluorescence quenching. For bio-chemical system of g-C3N4-levosimendan, electron-hole theory explains the electron system well in the excited states. Consequently, with the help of molecular orbital theory, it enables us to differentiate between electron donor and electron acceptor. For absorptive process of g-C3N4-levosimendan complex, we have plotted diagrams of electron-hole for PET process for n-states of 1 to 5 [99,100]. These diagrams along with various transitions and wavelengths are shown in Fig. 14 . The wavelength of absorption for first state has a value of 346 nm while for the fifth state it came out to be 295 nm while for second, third and fourth states the absorption wavelengths occurred at 345 nm, 333 nm and 313 nm, respectively. In Fig. 14, the molecular orbitals for hole and electrons can be seen in magenta and green colors.

For detailed evaluation, some parameters are observed and mentioned in Table 8 . The “D” is the distance between electron-hole orbitals. Greater the distance greater will be the PCT lengths. It has been depicted from the table that for fourth state the D is larger while for state 3 it is smaller representing the localized excitations.

The overlap between electron- hole orbitals represented by S in Table 8 demonstrate the rate at which charge transfer takes place. All the values of S for first five states are circa 0.5 a.u. representing a somewhat higher charge-transfer rate. Quenching phenomena occurs where the electron hole transition is seen from levosimendan to graphitic carbon. Thus, we can say that the complex formation has resulted in quenching. It is a phase wherein the automated excitation wavelength of an excited molecule is shifted to a quencher molecule through charge transfer resulting in the quencher molecule emitting non-fluorescently [101]. In g-C3N4-levosimendan complex, the g-C3N4 act as a chelator while levosimendan as a fluorophore and charge transfer occurs from fluorophore to chelator. The transitions LP (1) N15 → σ*C9-N13 show a decrease from 17.68 kcal mol−1 to 16.86 kcal mol−1 while for LP (1) N39 → σ*C40-N46 the decrease is seen from 5.63 kcal mol−1 to 5.48 kcal mol−1. Likewise, the transition energy for LP (1) N45 → σ*C10-N14 after g-C3N4-levosimendan complex formation rises from 1.64 to 1.87 kcal mol−1 while for σ C17- N23 → σ* N24-C28 from 1.99 kcal mol−1 to 2.02 kcal mol−1.

For locating electron-rich and electron-poor areas on the complex g-C3N4-levosimendan, electrostatic potential is mapped. The charge allocation on g-C3N4-levosimendan is demonstrated by coloring patterns wherein red color indicates maximum electron density cloud, blue color points out positively charged ESP and green color signifies electron deficient region. In Fig. 15 , it has been clearly seen that the red color appears where the interaction of drug and carrier takes place. Furthermore, the ESP plot demonstrates that the red color appeared on drug levosimendan indicating acceptor behavior. Here the donor unit has been indicated by blue color which can be viewed at the g-C3N4 surface whereas green color signifies the region with no charge. The ESP estimation confirms the flow of electron density from carrier g-C3N4 to levosimendan which supports the above results.

Discovery of new therapeutics and their administration challenges have resulted in an advanced drug delivery system. In this study we analyze the therapeutic efficacy of graphitic carbon nitride for the successful preferential targeted delivery of a cardiovascular drug; levosimendan. As modeling almost produces the same results to that of experimental values, so we use TD-DFT method to compute the quantum properties of the drug (levosimendan), carrier (graphitic carbon nitride) and their complex (g-C3N4-levosimendan) at ground state as well as at excited phase. The BSSE corrected adsorption energy value obtained for complex signifies the successful exothermic adsorption reaction. The formation of HOMO on g-C3N4 and that of LUMO on levosimendan confirms the charge transfer phenomena which is also explained by ESP. The dipole moment in water (polar) solutions show a drastic increase in solubility. Other chemical reactivity parameters also confirm the adsorption process. The significant effect of dispersion on adsorption has been seen and the presence of weak, non-covalent intermolecular forces like Van der Waal’s forces is further confirmed by visual representation of NCI plot which further confirms the successful off-loading of drug at the target tissue. Natural bond orbital analysis explains the different type of transitions occurred from g-C3N4 to levosimendan or levosimendan to g-C3N4. PET process is successfully explained based on electron-hole theory, for different excited states. The maximum absorption wavelengths obtained for g-C3N4, levosimendan and their complex show results close to that of experimental values. Moreover, the charge effect study explains less distortion and reveals the active spots of the complex molecule. In simple words it is concluded that the carrier g-C3N4 shows good co-ordination with the cardiovascular drug; levosimendan and can be used to deliver levosimendan to the specific target tissue. The promising frontiers of g-C3N4 (as discussed in this paper) are helpful in future as it has the potential for incredible medical use and opens a new horizon for researchers to deal with the questions related to drug delivery.
